idioms.idiom: life and soul of the party
idioms.definition: The most lively, entertaining, and sociable person at a gathering or social event
examples: He's always the life and soul of the party – everyone loves being around him.
She turned up late but immediately became the life and soul of the party.
My uncle was the life and soul of every family gathering, always telling jokes.
idioms.usageTip: Always positive. Describes someone energetic and entertaining who makes social events more enjoyable for everyone. Can also be used slightly ironically.

idioms.idiom: like two peas in a pod
idioms.definition: Very similar to each other in appearance, personality, or behaviour
examples: The twins are like two peas in a pod – I can never tell them apart.
Those two have been like two peas in a pod since primary school.
My sister and I are like two peas in a pod: we even finish each other's sentences.
idioms.usageTip: Refers to strong similarity, usually in personality or appearance. Always used about two people. The comparison is neutral or affectionate.

idioms.definition: Having a friendly, civil, or pleasant relationship with someone
examples: Even after the divorce, they remained on good terms for the sake of their children.
It's important to stay on good terms with your neighbours.
She's on good terms with all her former colleagues.
idioms.usageTip: Implies a relationship that is at least amicable, even if not a close friendship. Also used in the negative: 'not on good terms'. Compare with 'on bad terms'.

idioms.definition: Going out to restaurants, bars, and clubs to enjoy yourself, typically at night
examples: It was her birthday, so they decided to go out on the town.
After passing their exams, the students were out on the town until midnight.
We don't go on the town much anymore now that we have kids.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used with 'go out on the town' or 'a night on the town'. Implies enjoying city nightlife and entertainment. Slightly informal.

idioms.definition: Your close family members or blood relatives
examples: How can you turn away your own flesh and blood in their time of need?
She would do anything for her flesh and blood.
They may not always get along, but they're flesh and blood, and that matters.
idioms.usageTip: Used to emphasise the importance of family ties, often in emotional contexts. Can imply a moral obligation to support or forgive a family member.

idioms.definition: In a situation where someone is displeased with you (bad books) or pleased with you (good books)
examples: I forgot our anniversary, so I'm firmly in her bad books.
He stayed late to finish the report and is now in his manager's good books.
Try not to get in the boss's bad books during your first week.
idioms.usageTip: Used informally in both professional and personal contexts. 'Bad books' is more common than 'good books'. The origin is from record-keeping of debtors.

fillInExercises.sentence: As always, Marco was ___ – dancing, telling stories, and making everyone laugh.
options: the life and soul of the party
fillInExercises.correct: the life and soul of the party
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Life and soul of the party' describes the most energetic and entertaining person at a social event.

fillInExercises.sentence: Those two cousins are ___. Same sense of humour, same taste in music, same everything.
options: like two peas in a pod
fillInExercises.correct: like two peas in a pod
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Like two peas in a pod' means very similar in personality or appearance.

fillInExercises.sentence: Despite a difficult split, the former business partners stayed ___ and even referred clients to each other.
options: like two peas in a pod
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On good terms' means maintaining a friendly or civil relationship with someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: We're celebrating tonight – a proper night ___ with dinner and dancing.
options: like two peas in a pod
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On the town' means going out to enjoy restaurants, bars, and clubs, typically in the evening.

fillInExercises.sentence: No matter what he's done, he's still your ___, and family should stick together.
options: life and soul of the party
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Flesh and blood' refers to close family members, emphasising the bond of blood relations.

fillInExercises.sentence: I forgot to submit the report on time, so I'm definitely ___ this week.
options: like two peas in a pod
fillInExercises.explanation: 'In someone's bad books' means that person is displeased or disappointed with you.

lines.speaker: Beth
lines.text: Your cousin Thomas was absolutely [the life and soul of the party|life and soul of the party] at the barbecue yesterday.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: He always is. His friend Jake is the same – those two are [like two peas in a pod|like two peas in a pod].

lines.speaker: Beth
lines.text: I noticed your aunt wasn't there. Are you still [on good terms|on good terms] with her?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Just about. Things got awkward last year, but she's [your flesh and blood|your flesh and blood], so we're making the effort.

lines.speaker: Beth
lines.text: That's the right attitude. By the way, did you hear Thomas is planning a proper night [on the town|on the town] for his birthday next month?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Yes, he mentioned it. I hope I'm still [in his good books|in sb's good books] after I missed his last birthday dinner.

lines.speaker: Beth
lines.text: You are – he said you're forgiven. Just make sure you don't double-book this time!

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: I heard the new intern is brilliant. Apparently she's already [in the director's good books|in sb's good books].

lines.speaker: Liam
lines.text: Really? Her supervisor says the same. They're [like two peas in a pod|like two peas in a pod] – same work style, same drive.

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: Good for her. I'm still trying to get back [on good terms|on good terms] with finance after the budget dispute last quarter.

lines.speaker: Liam
lines.text: I'm sure you'll get there. On a lighter note – are you coming to the team social on Friday? We're going [on the town|on the town].

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: I'd love to. I could do with a laugh. Is Pete coming? He's always [the life and soul of the party|life and soul of the party].

lines.speaker: Liam
lines.text: He is, yes. His partner too. Those two are [in each other's good books|in sb's good books] at the moment – they've been getting on brilliantly.

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: Ha, I know what you mean. Right, count me in for Friday.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.idiom: life and soul of the party
pairs.meaning: the most lively and entertaining person at a social event

pairs.idiom: like two peas in a pod
pairs.meaning: very similar in appearance or character
having a friendly, pleasant relationship with someone
going out to enjoy restaurants, bars, and clubs
your close family or blood relatives
in a situation where someone is pleased or displeased with you

quizExercises.sentence: Even after their argument, the two colleagues stayed ___ and continued to collaborate professionally.
options: like two peas in a pod
quizExercises.explanation: 'On good terms' means maintaining a civil and friendly relationship even after a disagreement.

quizExercises.sentence: She forgot to cc her manager on a key email – she's been ___ ever since.
options: like two peas in a pod
the life and soul of the party
quizExercises.explanation: 'In someone's bad books' means that person is unhappy or disappointed with you.

quizExercises.idiom: life and soul of the party
quizExercises.question: Which person is the 'life and soul of the party'?
options: Someone who sits quietly in a corner checking their phone all evening
Someone who gets everyone laughing, dancing, and talking throughout the event
Someone who arrives on time and helps tidy up at the end
quizExercises.explanation: 'Life and soul of the party' describes someone who energises the whole event and makes it enjoyable for everyone.

quizExercises.question: When would you most likely use the phrase 'your flesh and blood'?
options: When encouraging someone to support or forgive a family member
When describing a close friendship between two colleagues
When talking about a stranger who needs help
quizExercises.explanation: 'Your flesh and blood' refers to close family members and is often used to appeal to a sense of family loyalty or duty.
